ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tomato beef rice as a composite Singaporean dish; mapped to closest parent 'beef dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tomato beef rice; A0CKM (beef meat dish) used as the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for tomato beef rice dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tomato beef with rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of braised tomato beef (roughly 150 g) served over a standard plate of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g).

nutrient_source.title: Tomato Beef Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tomato Beef Rice (cr_id G00011063). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Fields not reported by HPB (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, water) are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tomato beef rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) substantially diluted by the high-protein beef and tomato components, which are known to lower the composite meal GI. The mixed-meal GI is estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted blending principle consistent with Brand-Miller et al. methodology. The carbohydrate content of the dish is low (4.19 g/100g), yielding a glycemic load per 350 g serving of approximately 4.0 (very low). Composite meal GI estimation is consistent with published mixed-meal GI literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 369 mg/100g with partially offsetting potassium 368 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content of 369 mg/100g is in the borderline-high range for hypertension management; patients should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake, noting that the high potassium-to-sodium ratio (approximately 1:1) provides partial DASH-diet benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.19 g/100g), high protein (18.48 g/100g), low sugar (1.45 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The very low carbohydrate and sugar content combined with high protein makes this dish favourable for glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes; however, patients should monitor total portion size as the rice base contributes additional carbohydrate load beyond the per-100g values reported, and substituting brown rice for white rice will further improve the glycaemic and fibre profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) with cholesterol 52 mg/100g and moderate sodium 369 mg/100g; saturated fat data unavailable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef is a source of saturated fat and dietary cholesterol (52 mg/100g), both of which are relevant to LDL-cholesterol management in cardiovascular disease; patients should limit portion frequency to 2–3 times per week, request minimal added oil during cooking, and consider substituting beef with lean chicken or fish to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (arachidonic acid, omega-6) and tomato (nightshade solanine) are pro-inflammatory in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; tomato is a nightshade vegetable that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals, and patients with active RA should consider substituting beef with oily fish and monitoring symptom response to tomato consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Beef with Rice (番茄牛肉饭, Fān qié niú ròu fàn) is a popular Singaporean Chinese comfort dish consisting of tender braised or stir-fried beef slices cooked with ripe tomatoes in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and ginger, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and Chinese-style economy rice (cai fan) stalls. The beef is typically thinly sliced and marinated before being wok-fried or braised until tender, while the tomatoes break down into a tangy, slightly sweet sauce that coats the meat. The flavour profile is savoury-umami with a mild tomato acidity and a hint of sweetness from the oyster sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is notable for its high protein content (18.48 g/100g) and relatively low carbohydrate content (4.19 g/100g) when measured as the beef-tomato component alone, though the rice base adds significant carbohydrate load in a full serving. Sodium is moderately elevated at 369 mg/100g due to soy and oyster sauce, but is partially offset by a high potassium content of 368 mg/100g. The dish provides meaningful iron (1.84 mg/100g) and magnesium (23 mg/100g) from the beef. Clinically, it is suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its low glycaemic load, but requires sodium monitoring for hypertension and moderated frequency for cardiovascular disease due to red meat cholesterol and saturated fat content. It is not recommended for individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of beef and the nightshade classification of tomatoes.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00011063, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The record is marked as canonical and reviewed in the source system. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated via analogous-food methodology from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of the accessed date. Detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin micronutrients, and moisture data are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this composite dish entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on the carbohydrate-weighted mixed-meal blending principle; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is not certified halal as beef sourcing and sauce ingredients (oyster sauce) may vary by hawker stall. Saturated fat data unavailable from HPB; cardiovascular disease guidance is based on general red meat composition knowledge from USDA FoodData Central beef entries as supporting context.
